front: Event: an army led by the general Abu Muslim fought under the Black Standard and captured Merv
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: decided when Abu Muslim's forces defeated Marwan II at the Battle of the Zab
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: fueled by mawali converts angered by harsh kharaj and jizya taxes in Khurasan
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: its leader took the title as-Saffah ("the Blood-Shedder") upon entering Kufa
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: caused the word dawla to shift in meaning from "turn" to "dynasty"
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: preceded by decades of secret Hashimite organizing, including missionaries known as the "twelve naqibs"
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: a losing Umayyad prince fled after horsemen left his decapitated brother's body to rot near a river
work: The Abbasid Revolution

front: Event: the magus Sunbadh revolted after Abu Muslim's execution, claiming Abu Muslim had become a dove in a brass fortress
work: Early Revolts: Sunbadh and al-Muqanna "the Veiled One"

front: Event: led by "the Veiled One," whose "red-wearing" followers believed the spirit of God existed in a general executed by the dynasty
work: Early Revolts: Sunbadh and al-Muqanna "the Veiled One"

front: Event: its leader became known as "the abominable one" after winning the Day of the Barges
work: Early Revolts: Sunbadh and al-Muqanna "the Veiled One"

front: Ruler: sent Charlemagne a water clock, ivory chessmen, and an elephant named Abul-Abbas
work: Harun al-Rashid and the Fall of the Barmakids

front: Ruler: founded the House of Wisdom in Baghdad
work: Harun al-Rashid and the Fall of the Barmakids

front: Figure: this family of Iranian viziers, said to descend from Buddhist leaders of Balkh, advised the dynasty early in Harun al-Rashid's reign
work: Harun al-Rashid and the Fall of the Barmakids

front: Figure: this family's power was destroyed by Harun al-Rashid after one of its members reportedly impregnated his sister Abbasa
work: Harun al-Rashid and the Fall of the Barmakids

front: Institution: built by al-Mansur; center of the dynasty's mass translation of Greek texts into Arabic
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Scholar: wrote the Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing while working at the House of Wisdom, developing early algebra
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Work: described over a hundred automata, including a programmable flute player and a "haunted bottle" that dispensed two different liquids
back: Book of Ingenious Devices (Banu Musa brothers)
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Work: the first comprehensive treatment of optics, produced under Abbasid patronage
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Concept: an Abbasid-era scholar's On Burning Mirrors and Lenses gave the first mathematical description of this law of refraction
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Work: this geography chronicled world history from Adam and Eve up to the Abbasid dynasty
back: Meadows of Gold and the Mines of Gems (al-Masudi)
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Figure: this secret philosophical society's 52 epistles formed the first proto-encyclopedia
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Concept: this dynasty's later peripatetic school called Aristotle the "First Teacher" and the author of The Virtuous City the "Second Teacher"
work: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement

front: Event: launched by al-Ma'mun to enforce the Mu'tazilite doctrine that the Qur'an was created rather than co-eternal with God
work: The Mihna and Mu'tazilite Controversy

front: Figure: this mystic angered an Abbasid ruler after declaring "I am the Truth"
work: The Mihna and Mu'tazilite Controversy

front: Figure: inaugurated a scholarly tradition of Qur'anic exegesis (tafsir) in the 9th century, during which the Mu'tazili school thrived
work: The Mihna and Mu'tazilite Controversy

front: Event: a revolt of enslaved Black plantation laborers in Basra, led by Ali ibn Muhammad, who claimed descent from the caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib
work: The Zanj Rebellion and the Qarmatians

front: Figure: chronicled the Zanj Rebellion in his History of the Prophets and Kings
work: The Zanj Rebellion and the Qarmatians

front: Event: the rebel Rayhan was among its leaders, and a related "anarchy" was partly ended by Musa ibn Bugha before a further revolt of salt-producing slaves
work: The Zanj Rebellion and the Qarmatians

front: Institution: this syncretic Ismaili Shi'a sect, which set up a utopian state in Bahrain under Abu-Tahir Sulayman, sacked Mecca in 930 and ransomed the Ka'aba's Black Stone
work: The Zanj Rebellion and the Qarmatians

front: Place: al-Mu'tasim moved the Abbasid capital here in 836 after tensions between Turkic soldiers and Arab residents
work: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"

front: Place: al-Mutawakkil built this city's Great Mosque, with a spiral-ramped minaret, before being murdered here by his own Turkic bodyguards
work: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"

front: Period: began with the death of al-Muntasir and saw al-Mu'taz eventually succeed his cousin al-Musta'in
work: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"

front: Period: this decade of instability weakened the caliphate enough to allow Ahmad ibn Tulun's rise in Egypt
work: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"

front: Concept: Turkic slave-soldiers purchased from Transoxiana, used by al-Ma'mun and al-Mu'tasim in wars against the Byzantine Empire
work: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"

front: Battle: the Abbasids defeated the Tang dynasty after Karluk mercenaries defected from Chinese general Gao Xianzhi
work: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China

front: Battle: this 751 engagement is credited (per al-Tabari) with spreading papermaking westward, leading to the first non-Chinese paper mill at Samarqand
work: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China

front: Event: Abbasid ruler Abu Jafar sent mercenaries to help the Tang dynasty suppress this 8th-century Chinese rebellion
work: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China

front: Concept: in 750 CE, the Abbasid and Tang capitals were the world's two most populous cities
work: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China

front: Concept: Abbasid and Tang emissaries called each other "those with the black robes" because of their dress
work: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China

front: Event: the last Abbasid caliph was executed by being rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses
work: The Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)

front: Figure: this grandson of Genghis Khan, founder of the Ilkhanate, sacked Baghdad in 1258 and destroyed the House of Wisdom
work: The Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)

front: Event: the Tigris River allegedly ran black with ink from the destroyed House of Wisdom's manuscripts
work: The Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)

front: Battle: halted Hulagu Khan's westward advance after the Mongols were defeated by the Mamluks
work: The Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)

front: Figure: this qiyan singer, purchased by al-Ma'mun, defeated the qayna Shariya in a singing contest at Samarra
work: The Qiyan: Enslaved Court Musicians

front: Concept: enslaved female singer-courtesans in Abbasid high society, roughly analogous to later Japanese geisha
work: The Qiyan: Enslaved Court Musicians
